title: Quantum critical collapse of a pinned vortex glass
authors: David Perconte, Thibault Charpentier, Nikolaos Koutsopoulos, Kalpajit Roy, Nadjib Benchabane, Xiaoli Peng, Florent Blondelle, Frédéric Gay, Mikhail Feigel'man, Viktor Kabanov, Benjamin Sacépé
abstract: The interplay between disorder and vortex--vortex interactions in strongly disordered superconductors in a magnetic field can stabilize a vortex-glass state, characterized by strong pinning and the absence of positional order. Yet its role in the destruction of superconductivity at the field-driven superconductor--insulator transition has remained unresolved. Here we use plasmonic microwave spectroscopy of superconducting resonators patterned from amorphous indium oxide thin films to directly track the superfluid density up to the critical field $B_c$. We find an unexpected resilience of the superfluid density, which decreases only logarithmically over nearly three orders of magnitude in field, in stark contrast to the rapid power-law suppression expected for vortex lattices. We attribute this anomalously slow decay to a collective vortex-pinning mechanism counterintuitively enhanced by vortex--vortex interactions. The superfluid density then vanishes linearly at $B_c$, where independent magnetoresistance measurements identify a continuous quantum critical point, unlike the abrupt transition observed at zero field. We further uncover an exceptionally large nonlinear electromagnetic response of the vortex glass, manifested as a pronounced positive-Kerr effect with potential for quantum sensing. These results show how disorder controls the critical magnetic field and identify the vortex glass as the key intermediate state governing the magnetic-field-induced superconductor--insulator transition.
summary: This paper demonstrates that in strongly disordered superconductors, the superfluid density is unexpectedly resilient to magnetic fields, decaying only logarithmically due to a collective pinning effect. It identifies a continuous quantum critical point at the transition to the insulator. The discovery of a positive Kerr effect in the vortex glass also suggests potential applications in quantum sensing.
main_problem: Understanding the mechanism behind the magnetic-field-driven superconductor-insulator transition (SIT) in strongly disordered superconductors and how the vortex glass state influences the collapse of superfluid stiffness.
main_result: The superfluid density decreases only logarithmically with the magnetic field due to a collective vortex-pinning mechanism where vortex-vortex interactions enhance pinning, eventually vanishing linearly at a continuous quantum critical point.
method: The study combines plasmonic microwave spectroscopy of superconducting resonators with simultaneous DC transport measurements and numerical simulations of 2D logarithmically interacting particles in a random potential.
model_or_system: The physical system consists of amorphous indium oxide (a:InO) thin films in a high-disorder, high-magnetic-field regime, modeled as a 2D assembly of vortices in a random pinning potential with logarithmic interactions.
key_observables: Superfluid stiffness (extracted from kinetic inductance), sheet resistance, critical current density, internal quality factor, and microwave transmission (S21).
important_parameters: Magnetic field (B), critical field (Bc), disorder strength (W), temperature (T ~ 10 mK), and vortex density (n/nc).
main_assumptions: The London approximation for vortex interactions fails at high densities; the system is treated as effectively 2D due to the thin film geometry.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the resonator device and microwave transmission; Figure 2 displays superfluid stiffness decay across different disorder levels; Figure 3 shows magnetoresistance isotherms and the linear approach to the critical field; Figure 4 illustrates the positive Kerr nonlinearity and power-dependent dissipation.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of the SIT, presents experimental microwave spectroscopy results showing anomalous logarithmic decay, proposes a 'cage model' for interaction-enhanced pinning, validates this with numerical simulations of vortex glasses, and concludes with observations of nonlinear electromagnetic responses.
why_interesting: The study explores the dynamics of a many-body system (vortex glass) subject to competing random potentials and long-range interactions, which is highly relevant to the study of localization and collective dynamics in disordered many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Superconducting plasmons in the mixed state. a, Device schematic. L-shaped a:InO resonators (grey) are capacitively coupled to a 50 Ωimpedance-matched gold feedline (center). The magnetic field is applied perpendicular to the substrate. An additional a:InO mesa, contacted by four DC probes, is used for resistance mea- surements (top). The inset illustrates the random pinning potential W(r) and the resulting vortex glass, represented as black circles. b, c, Microwave transmission S21, normalized to its normal-state value above the critical field, as a function of frequency and magnetic field for resonators DPRes2a and DPRes2b (b), and DPRes11a and DPRes11c (c). In b, higher harmonics...

FIG. 2: Interaction-induced collective pinning of the vortex glass. Normalized superfluid stiffness Θ(B)/Θ(0) as a function of normalized magnetic field B/BΘ c . Colors correspond to different disorder realizations (characterized by their sheet inductance): DPRes2a (red), DPRes9a (green), and DPRes11a (blue). DPRes2a and DPRes11a were measured with microwave powers of −103 dBm at low field and −83 dBm at high field, while DPRes9a was measured at −103 dBm throughout. The corresponding critical fields are BΘ c = 9.6 T, 5.6 T, and 0.44 T for DPRes2a, DPRes9a, and DPRes11a, respectively. FIG. 3: Magnetic-field-driven superconductor-insulator transition. a, Sheet resistance versus magnetic field measured in a current-bias configuration (0.1–1 nA) for samples DPRes9 and DPRes13 at temperatures ranging from 10 mK (blue) to 400 mK (red) in 100 mK steps. The anomalous kink in the 10 mK curve of DPRes9 is reproducible and could originate from low-temperature sensitivity to inhomogeneous current paths [23] in the narrow strip of the mesa Hall bar. b, Sheet resistance of the same samples measured in voltage bias, together with the corresponding superfluid stiffness (blue triangles and green circles) extracted from the on-chip resonators, as a function of magnetic field. The...

FIG. 4: Positive Kerr nonlinearity and dissipation. a, c, Microwave transmission S21, in arbitrary units, as a function of photon number and frequency shift δf/f relative to the low-photon-number resonance frequency, at B = 0 T (a) and B = 0.3 T (c), for sample DPRes8. b, Relative frequency shift δf/f versus photon number, extracted at various magnetic fields. d, Internal quality factor Qi versus photon number, at various magnetic fields; the legend is the same as in b. In b and d, filled black circles correspond to measurements performed after zero-field cooling, whereas the open black circle corresponds to a measurement with trapped vortices. Measurements were performed at T = 0.01 K.
